It’s  exhausting how war movies keep portraying American soldiers as heroes who are just saving lives and doing the right thing—when we know that’s not the full story. You don’t get to invade someone’s country, bomb their cities, kill civilians and children, and then act surprised when there’s retaliation. Actions have consequences.

Meanwhile, look at the U.S. today—homelessness is out of control, people are drowning in medical bills, and jobs barely pay enough to survive. How can anyone still see America as a global ‘beacon of hope’ when its own citizens are being neglected?

Let’s be real—the U.S. doesn’t go to war to protect freedom. It goes to secure resources, destabilize regions, and keep feeding the military-industrial complex. Just look at Iraq—there were no weapons of mass destruction. That was a lie. But the oil contracts and defense deals? Very real. War is business. And Hollywood’s role in glorifying it is just part of the machine.

This is the most accurate war film I've ever seen. At the beginning I was giddy ready for an action flick, but by the end of it I was watching it as more of a documentary. Almost like it was filmed during the events. Everything was flawless. It really took me back. The uniforms, the way people behave under stress in combat, the SOPs, the lingo, the behavior, the little details like gear accountability, I mean everything was depicted just like real life. There was no soundtrack. There was no clear cut story. It was just an op gone wrong from start to finish. No nonsense or filler.  This is not Blackhawk Down. This is not Saving Private Ryan. This is a very methodical movie that portrays how combat occurred during the GWOT down to the very minute details.  

Honestly this movie probably won't win any awards, but if you're interested in seeing what the war in Iraq is really like, go see this film. It's solid. I give it a solid 10/10. If you're a vet, go see this if you can handle it. This is a movie by vets for vets.

The President of the United States of America takes pleasure in presenting the Silver Star to Special Warfare Operator First Class (SEAL) Ray Mendoza, United States Navy, for conspicuous gallantry and intrepidity in action against the enemy while serving as Lead Communicator, Naval Special Warfare Task Unit-Ramadi, in direct support of Operation IRAQI FREEDOM on 19 November 2006. During a Sniper over-watch mission in the dangerous city of Ramadi, Iraq, Petty Officer Mendoza’s position was attacked with grenade and small arms fire from multiple enemy locations, wounding one SEAL. Upon moving the wounded SEAL to the extract platform, there was a massive explosion, killing one Iraqi Army scout and wounding seven other members of the element. With total disregard for his own life, Petty Officer Mendoza heroically left the safety of his fortified position to engage multiple enemy positions. He advanced into the middle of the street under heavy enemy fire to drag one of the two severely wounded SEALs away from the coordinated enemy ambush. Petty Officer Mendoza then rendered life saving medical care to the wounded SEAL, stopping massive blood loss from multiple wounds and compound fractures. Upon arrival of the casualty evacuation platform, he carried the wounded SEAL through withering enemy fire to the safety of the extract vehicle. Petty Officer Mendoza’s bold leadership, courageous actions, and complete devotion to duty reflected great credit upon him and were in keeping with the highest traditions of the United States Naval Service.

Two things I will mention:

1. The better the movie, the more fucked up I walk out of the theater.  This one looks to have me processing my PTSD for a solid week.  I hope it's as real as the trailer portrays.

2. I was in Iraq 04-05, as a medic for an infantry unit.  I saw my fair share of shit, but luckily nothing like what's shown here.  Most of us talked about how pointless us being there was.  Most of us talked about how us being there was wrong.  How the tactics used to "win hearts and minds" was complete horseshit.  But I will also say that the VAST majority of the men I served with and other units we ran into were professionals.  As stupid as our mission was, we did the very best to carry it out with honor and dignity.  Did I personally see potential results of war crimes committed by Blackwater mercenary thugs and other undisciplined National Guard and Reservist units who were scared and trigger happy and would shoot anything that moved?  Absolutely.  But the amount of couch potato cowards typing in the comments over-simplifying an extremely complex conflict and using terms they have no fucking clue about is laughable.  Maybe that kind of rhetoric works on hippie chicks and impresses your friends, but anyone with prior service and common sense understands that the men there didn't want to be there, but had a job to do.

When I was a kid my father and I would watch "Combat" and it drove him nuts. He served in the Marines for 26 years, fought in WWII and Korea (Silver Stars and Purple Hearts). He would shout at the TV about how you wouldn't do this or shouldn't do that, same with "War" movies. I used to wish he'd not comment so much, I thought the guys filming these must have been combat veterans themselves, at least somebody on the set had been. Then I joined the Army and went off to Vietnam. When I came back after my first tour, he and I smoked a cigarette out on the lanai, drank a beer together and cried together. We cried because we were sad, and proud and because we were laughing at the funny shit that happens sometimes. It was the first time I ever saw my father cry, but then we laughed ourselves sick telling stories to each other. Each time I came home, my mother was a wreck and my father and I laughed and drank beer (never more than two). Now, I'm a lot older and I do the same thing. My wife tells me "if you don't like what they do in the movies, why watch them?" and she has a point. I've stopped watching war movies...and I'm okay. I see these "Trailers" and I can hear my father roaring "Cover fire, don't you assholes know how to lay down covering fire?".

It’s disheartening to realize that most people watching movies like this rarely question the narratives they promote. The United States has never experienced war on its own soil in the way many other nations have. Instead, every time it engages in conflict, it involves invading other countries, disrupting their homes, cultures, and way of life. Billions of dollars in U.S. tax revenue are spent annually on wars that result in the deaths of countless civilians and children, while millions of Americans grapple with drug addiction, abuse, poverty, and homelessness. The very act of invading a foreign land—so vastly different in culture, language, and geography—should, in itself, raise serious questions about the morality and implications of such actions.

I’m done with war movies made by the West. They’re little more than propaganda, consistently framing people from the Middle East and such as the villains. If more people took the time to read history books and broaden their perspectives, they’d start to see the hidden motives behind these portrayals—and perhaps recognize the role these narratives play in justifying endless cycles of violence and exploitation.

Hello from Iraq, I want to clarify something. The Iraqi people have three main sects: Shiite Muslims, who constitute the majority, Sunni Muslims, and Kurds. Saddam killed Shiite clerics such as Mohammed al-Hakim, Mohammed al-Sadr, al-Shirazi and others, executed many Shiite opponents, bombed the shrine of Imam Hussein, who is a holy person for the Shiites, and killed the Kurds with chemical weapons in Halabja. The Shiites constitute the overwhelming.  Saddam killed thousands of civilians on various charges, whether as an opponent or someone who expressed his opinion ،majority in nine provinces, and the Kurds constitute a complete majority in three provinces in the north, which are Dohuk, Sulaymaniya and Erbil. There are two equal provinces, Baghdad and Diyala, and only three Sunni provinces. The Shiites believe that Saddam wronged them, and they say that now is better than Saddam’s rule, and the Kurds do the same, while the Sunnis say that Saddam’s rule was better (Saddam was a Sunni Muslim). Therefore, when you go to Karbala province now and ask someone there if it is better now or during Saddam’s rule, he will say now because he is a Shiite Muslim. The same answer is given when you ask a Kurdish citizen. Iraq was better as a regional political power, but economically it was a failure, and we were ruled by a one-party dictatorial regime, which is Baath Party. Now we have many parties and the freedom to elect any candidate, salaries are now much higher and the economy is better now we eat everything while during Saddam's rule the people couldn't buy Pepsi because it was expensive and most of the people have modern cars now. As I mentioned before it was better only from the political point of view as a regional power and a strong political system

Did 3 tours in Iraq. First 2 were the worst time to be there. I was a Bradley mechanic and recovery operator. Second tour I was sent to support 1/32 Cav 101st ABN when they got 13 Bradley's. Yeah 101st ABN with Bradley's lol. Of the 13 they got. 11 were destroyed or coded out for IED damage. I towed all but 1 back in as a recovery operator. 2 still burning as I towed them back in. 1 hit a AT mine right in front of me as I was out to recover another that blew a oil line. Crazy place to be back then. I was doing so many vehicle repairs and recovery missions and missions outside the wire that I honestly remember it all as a blur. There was just so much happening. I still to this day can't sleep at night because my mind is still waiting for recovery missions to come in late at night. When the crews were doing their patrols. lol it just never goes away.
